Article.ArticleTitle: ESPEN practical guideline on ethical aspects of medical nutrition therapy.

Abstract.AbstractText: The provision of medical nutrition therapy (MNT) is a medical intervention that requires an indication, a therapeutic goal, and the consent of the patient. The decision-making process surrounding MNT often involves complex ethical considerations. The aim of this guideline is to provide practical recommendations on an ethical approach to the decision-making process in clinical nutrition. This practical guideline was developed by an international multidisciplinary working group. The practical, solely consensus-based guideline is based on and updates the 2016 ESPEN Guideline on Ethical Aspects of Artificial Nutrition and Hydration, which has been shortened, supplemented with flowcharts, and expanded to include 3 additional ethical statements in palliative care and 1 new statement concerning human rights in clinical nutrition. Two Delphi rounds and a consensus conference were held to reach consensus. The guideline offers a critical summary for clinicians and caregivers regarding the ethics of MNT in special situations, describes common ethical dilemmas, and proposes an algorithm for the decision-making process. The provision of MNT must be undertaken in the patient's best interests, for their benefit, improvement, or cure. MNT should not expose the patient to disproportionate or unjustifiable risks, and must be indicated in line with current scientific data and guideline recommendations. If the benefit seems clear to the health care professional, the MNT must nevertheless be approved by the patient, who must be adequately informed of the aim and consequences. This guideline places particular emphasis on end-of-life issues, palliative medicine, dementia, and specific scenarios such as nursing care and intensive care. In a multicultural and pluralistic society faced with decisions to feed sick patients in special conditions, it is imperative that the reasoning and the course of action in clinical nutrition are presented in accordance with good clinical practice, adhering to ethical principles.

MedlineCitation.CoiStatement: Conflict of interest The expert members of the working group were accredited by the ESPEN Guidelines Group, the ESPEN Education and Clinical Practice Committee, and the ESPEN executive. All expert members have declared their individual conflicts of interest according to the rules of the International Committee of Medical Journal Editors (ICMJE). If potential conflicts were indicated, they were reviewed by the ESPEN guideline officers and, in cases of doubts, by the ESPEN executive. DC: honoraria for lectures from Fresenius Kabi, Nutricia, Nestlé Health Sciences, and ASDIA, Ethics Editor of Clinical Nutrition, Associate Editor of Clinical Nutrition; RB: nothing to declare; ER: payment or honoraria for lectures, presentations, speakers bureaus, manuscript writing or educational events from Pfizer; PB: nothing to declare; AB: ASPEN Board of advisors; ASPEN-Rhoads Research Foundation Board of Directors; Editorial Board- Nutrition in Clinical Practice; SCB: grants for conduction of clinical trials from Heilerde-Gesellschaft Luvos Just GmbH & Co. KG, Symbiopharm GmbH, and Lactopia GmbH, payment or honoraria for lectures etc. from Falk Foundation, Elly Lilly, German Society for Probiotic Medicine e. V. (DeProm), Takeda Pharma Vertrieb GmbH & Co. KG, and Janssen-Cilag GmbH, participation on a Data, Safety Monitoring Board or Advisory Board of Nestlé Health Science and MEDICE Inc, Associate Editor of Clinical Nutrition; CC: ESPEN general secretary; grants to the institution from Nestlé Health Science, Nutricia, Abbott, Nutrimed, Fresenius, consulting fees from Globalbodynet, Nutrisens, payment or honoraria for lectures from Fresenius, Takeda, Pisa, Nutrisens; LG: grants from Nestlé Health Science, Foundation Nutrition 2000Plus, Geneva Cancer League; consulting fees from Nestlé Health Science, Fresenius Kabi, Lilly; honoraria for lectures, presentations from Abbott; support for attending meetings and/or travel from Nestlé Health Science, Guideline officer of the European Society of Clinical Nutrition and Metabolism (ESPEN), member of the Continuing Education Committee of the Swiss Society of Clinical Nutrition and Metabolism (GESKES); KG: research support from Alcresta, and NorthSea Therapeutics, consultance for Otsuka Pharmaceutical Company, Fresenius Kabi, Mead Johnson, and NorthSea Therapeutics, royalties from UpToDate, and Wolters Kluwer, President of the American Society for Parenteral and Enteral Nutrition (ASPEN); MH: unrestricted research grants from Fresenius Kabi, Baxter and Abbott, payment or honoraria for lectures from Baxter, BBraun, Fresenius Kabi, Director of the nutritionDay project of ESPEN and Medical University Vienna; AKJ: grants from National Institutes of Health, Mirum Pharmaceuticals, Mid America Transplant Foundation, consulting fees from Mirum, ISPEN, American Rejent, Gilead, Camp4; FP: payment or honoraria for lectures from Takeda, Fresenius Kabi, Mayoly Spindler, AbbVie, Johnson & Johnson, Iconic, Celltrion; SK: speakers' honoraria from Baxter, Braun, Fresenius, Nestle, Nutricia, ESPEN Chairman; SS: grants to the institution from VectivBio, Nestlé Health Science, Dr. Falk, Sanofi, consulting fees from Baxter, Nestlé Health Science, Dr. Falk, Sanofi, Lilly, consulting fees from Pfizer, payment or honoraria for lectures etc. from Amgen, Fresenius Kabi, MSD, Nestlé Health Science, Nutricia, Thermo Fisher, Lilly, EverPharma, Takeda, Coloplast, Theradial, Viatris, support for attending meetings from Biocodex, ESPEN treasurer, SFNCM Vice-President;
